subject: Biology
coverageNote: Form directories exist only where teaching-module source PDFs were ingested. Missing forms must resolve as known-gap, not empty objects.

lesson.title: Assess strength of union and plant vigor.
topics: x) Ask groups to compare each other’s plants during the time frame.
 If you were a farmer, which crop would you propagate by budding and why?
activities: i) Put learners into small groups of 4-5
ii) Make all the necessary requirements available for the groups to use.
iii) Ask the groups to select a healthy, low-growing branch of the parent plant.
iv) Ask the groups to bend the branch down to the soil surface.
v) Ask the groups to make a small slit or wound on the underside of the branch (optional, to encourage rooting).
vi) Ask the groups to cover the wounded part with soil, leaving the tip of the branch exposed.
vii) Ask the groups to secure the branch with a peg or stone.
assessment: A school owns a small farm where they grow strawberries. They notice that new strawberry plants keep appearing around the
mother plant without them planting new seeds. These new plants are connected to the parent plant by thin stems that creep
(a) Identify the natural mode of vegetative propagation occurring in Maria’s farm.
(b) Explain how this method benefits the farmer in terms of crop yield and uniformity.
(c) What could be a limitation of relying only on this method for large-scale farming?
